revision.text: #redirect[[en:Mount_Tonomine]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]
多武峰山は元は談山神社の奥の院である談山妙楽寺の境内であったが、廃仏稀釈以降現在は談山神社となっている。西暦８００年代、巻向山手地方にあった[[神武天皇]]の御霊を、同寺に移したとされる。
多武峰山は元は談山神社の奥の院である談山妙楽寺の境内であったが、廃仏稀釈以降現在は談山神社となっている。西暦８００年代、巻向山手地方にあった[[神武天皇]]の御霊を、同寺に移したとされる。１５９６年慶長元年、同寺は郡山に遷され、[[曹洞宗]][[久松寺]]として現存する。

revision.comment: 強調。定義がよくわからない。
revision.text: '''多武峰'''山は元は談山神社の奥の院である談山妙楽寺の境内であったが、廃仏稀釈以降現在は談山神社となっている。西暦800年代、巻向山手地方にあった[[神武天皇]]の御霊を、同寺に移したとされる。[[1596年]]（[[慶長]]元年）、同寺は郡山に遷され、[[曹洞宗]][[久松寺]]として現存する。

'''多武峰''' (とおのみね) は[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]南部にある地区名である。元は談山神社の奥の院である談山妙楽寺の境内であったが、廃仏稀釈以降現在は談山神社となっている。西暦800年代、巻向山手地方にあった[[神武天皇]]の御霊を、同寺に移したとされる。[[1596年]]（[[慶長]]元年）、同寺は郡山に遷され、[[曹洞宗]][[久松寺]]として現存する。
[[Category:奈良県|とおのみね]]

'''多武峰''' (とおのみね) は[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]南部にある地区名である。元は談山神社の奥の院である談山妙楽寺の境内であったが、廃仏稀釈以降現在は談山神社となっている。西暦800年代、巻向山手地方にあった[[神武天皇]]の御霊を、同寺に移したとされる。[[1596年]]（[[慶長]]元年）、同寺は郡山に遷され、[[曹洞宗]][[久松寺 (大和郡山市)|久松寺]]として現存する。
[[Category:奈良県|とおのみね]]

'''多武峰''' (とおのみね) は[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]南部にある地区名である。元は談山神社の奥の院である談山妙楽寺の境内であったが、廃仏稀釈以降現在は談山神社となっている。西暦800年代、巻向山手地方にあった[[神武天皇]]の御霊を、同寺に移したとされる。[[1596年]]（[[慶長]]元年）、同寺は郡山に遷され、[[曹洞宗]][[久松寺 (大和郡山市)|久松寺]]として現存する。
談山神社は伝統行事「蹴鞠」で有名。
[[Category:奈良県|とおのみね]]

'''多武峰'''（とうのみね）は[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]南部にある山、および、その一帯にあった寺院のこと。明治初年の[[神仏分離]]により、[[談山神社]]となった。詳細は、[[談山神社]]を参照。'''多武峰街道'''は談山神社から等弥神社を経て[[山の辺の道]]に接続する。
== 多武峰にある主な社寺 == * [[談山神社]] * [[等弥神社]] * [[聖林寺]] ==交通アクセス== [[Image:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|県道155線。左側に歩行者に注意を促す標識がある]] 自動車の場合、従来は桜井方面より[[奈良県道37号桜井吉野線]]を上るしかなかったが、離合困難な一車線区間が多く、紅葉シーズンはひどく渋滞する。2009年に全線二車線の[[奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線]]が開通し、飛鳥方面からのアクセスが飛躍的に向上したが、 ハイキングコースと数カ所で交差しており、走行に注意が必要である。 == ギャラリー == 多武峰街道 <gallery> 画像:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|摩尼輪塔 画像:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|三社神社 画像:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|磨崖仏 画像:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|竹林 画像:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|不動の滝 画像:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|屋形橋 </gallery>
== その他 == * この付近にあるバス停の名前は、多武「'''峯'''」となっている。
== 関連項目 == *[[美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選]]
{{mountain-stub|pref=奈良県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とうのみね}} [[Category:桜井市]]

'''多武峰'''（とうのみね）は[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]南部にある山、および、その一帯にあった寺院のこと。明治初年の[[神仏分離]]により、[[談山神社]]となった。詳細は、[[談山神社]]を参照。'''多武峰街道'''は談山神社から等弥神社を経て[[山の辺の道]]に接続する。
== 多武峰にある主な社寺 == * [[談山神社]] * [[等弥神社]] * [[聖林寺]] ==交通アクセス== [[Image:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|県道155線。左側に歩行者への注意を促す標識がある]] 自動車の場合、従来は桜井方面より[[奈良県道37号桜井吉野線]]を上るしかなかったが、離合困難な一車線区間が多く、紅葉シーズンはひどく渋滞する。2009年に全線二車線の[[奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線]]が開通し、飛鳥方面からのアクセスが飛躍的に向上したが、 [[石舞台]]からのハイキングコースと数カ所で交差しており、走行に注意が必要である。いずれにせよ、多武峰の駐車場キャパシティに限界があるので、ハイシーズンは公共交通機関（[[桜井駅]]よりバス）ないし徒歩（上記ハイキングコースの場合、石舞台から1.5〜2時間程度）の利用が推奨される。 == ギャラリー == 多武峰街道 <gallery> 画像:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|摩尼輪塔 画像:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|三社神社 画像:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|磨崖仏 画像:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|竹林 画像:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|不動の滝 画像:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|屋形橋 </gallery>
== その他 == * この付近にあるバス停の名前は、多武「'''峯'''」となっている。
== 関連項目 == *[[美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選]]
{{mountain-stub|pref=奈良県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とうのみね}} [[Category:桜井市]]

'''多武峰'''（とうのみね）は[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]南部にある山、および、その一帯にあった寺院のこと。明治初年の[[神仏分離]]により、[[談山神社]]となった。詳細は、[[談山神社]]を参照。'''多武峰街道'''は談山神社から等弥神社を経て[[山の辺の道]]に接続する。
== 多武峰にある主な社寺 == * [[談山神社]] * [[等弥神社]] * [[聖林寺]]
== 交通アクセス == [[Image:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|県道155線。左側に歩行者への注意を促す標識がある]] 自動車の場合、従来は桜井方面より[[奈良県道37号桜井吉野線]]を上るしかなかったが、離合困難な一車線区間が多く、紅葉シーズンはひどく渋滞する。2009年に全線二車線の[[奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線]]が開通し、飛鳥方面からのアクセスが飛躍的に向上したが、 [[石舞台古墳|石舞台]]からのハイキングコースと数カ所で交差しており、走行に注意が必要である。いずれにせよ、多武峰の駐車場キャパシティに限界があるので、ハイシーズンは公共交通機関（[[桜井駅 (奈良県)|桜井駅]]よりバス）ないし徒歩（上記ハイキングコースの場合、石舞台から1.5〜2時間程度）の利用が推奨される。
== ギャラリー == 多武峰街道 <gallery> 画像:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|摩尼輪塔 画像:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|三社神社 画像:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|磨崖仏 画像:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|竹林 画像:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|不動の滝 画像:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|屋形橋 </gallery>
== その他 == * この付近にあるバス停の名前は、多武「'''峯'''」となっている。
== 関連項目 == * [[美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選]]
{{mountain-stub|pref=奈良県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とうのみね}} [[Category:桜井市]]

{{mountain-stub|pref=奈良県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とうのみね}} [[Category:桜井市]]

'''多武峰'''（とうのみね）は[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]南部にある山、および、その一帯にあった寺院のこと。明治初年の[[神仏分離]]により、[[談山神社]]となった。詳細は、[[談山神社]]を参照。'''多武峰街道'''は談山神社から等弥神社を経て[[山の辺の道]]に接続する。
== 社寺 == * [[談山神社]] * [[等弥神社]] * [[聖林寺]]
== 交通 == [[Image:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|県道155線。左側に歩行者への注意を促す標識がある]] 自動車の場合、従来は桜井方面より[[奈良県道37号桜井吉野線]]を上るしかなかったが、離合困難な一車線区間が多く、紅葉シーズンの渋滞は激しい。2009年に全線二車線の[[奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線]]が開通し、飛鳥方面からのアクセスが飛躍的に向上したが、[[石舞台古墳|石舞台]]からのハイキングコースと数カ所で交差しており、走行に注意が必要である。いずれにせよ、多武峰の駐車場キャパシティに限界があるので、ハイシーズンは公共交通機関（[[桜井駅 (奈良県)|桜井駅]]よりバス）ないし徒歩（上記ハイキングコースの場合、石舞台から1.5〜2時間程度）の利用が推奨される。

== その他 == * この付近にあるバス停の名前は、多武「'''峯'''」と表記されている。
== 関連項目 == * [[美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選]]

[[飛鳥時代]]に[[道教]]を信奉していた[[斉明天皇]]が、『日本書紀』に、「多武峰の山頂付近に石塁や高殿を築いて'''両槻宮'''（ふたつきのみや）とした」とある。『日本三代実録』に、[[858年]]（天安2年）「多武峰墓を藤原鎌足の墓とし、十陵四墓の例に入れる」と記されている。平安時代中頃の成立と見られる『多武峯略記』に、「最初は摂津国安威（現在の大阪府茨木市大織冠神社、[[阿武山古墳]]か）に葬られたが、後に大和国の多武峯に改葬された」との説が見える。

'''多武峰'''（とうのみね）は[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]南部にある山、および、その一帯にあった寺院のこと。
[[飛鳥時代]]に[[道教]]を信奉していた[[斉明天皇]]が、『日本書紀』に、「多武峰の山頂付近に石塁や高殿を築いて'''両槻宮'''（ふたつきのみや）とした」とある<ref>『[[日本書紀]]』では両槻宮を「観」と表現し、道教寺院を「道観」ということから道教施設説もあるが、「観」には単に「高殿」の意味もあるため、[[遠山美都男]]は疑問が残るとし、別名の「天宮」に関しても、道教[[仙人]]の住まう宮殿の意味だが、[[仏教]]の[[帝釈天]]が住む「忉利天宮」の略称の可能性も出す。[[遠山美都男]]『天皇と日本の起源「飛鳥の大王」の謎を解く』（[[講談社現代新書]]、2003年）p.186.</ref>。『日本三代実録』に、[[858年]]（天安2年）「多武峰墓を藤原鎌足の墓とし、十陵四墓の例に入れる」と記されている。平安時代中頃の成立と見られる『多武峯略記』に、「最初は摂津国安威（現在の大阪府茨木市大織冠神社、[[阿武山古墳]]か）に葬られたが、後に大和国の多武峯に改葬された」との説が見える。
明治初年の[[神仏分離]]により、[[談山神社]]となった。詳細は、[[談山神社]]を参照。'''多武峰街道'''は談山神社から等弥神社を経て[[山の辺の道]]に接続する。
== 社寺 == * [[談山神社]] * [[等弥神社]] * [[聖林寺]]
== 交通 == [[Image:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|県道155線。左側に歩行者への注意を促す標識がある]] 自動車の場合、従来は桜井方面より[[奈良県道37号桜井吉野線]]を上るしかなかったが、すれ違い困難な一車線区間が多く、紅葉シーズンの渋滞は激しい。2009年に全線二車線の[[奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線]]が開通し、飛鳥方面からのアクセスが飛躍的に向上したが、[[石舞台古墳|石舞台]]からのハイキングコースと数カ所で交差しており、走行に注意が必要である。いずれにせよ、多武峰の駐車場キャパシティに限界があるので、ハイシーズンは公共交通機関（[[桜井駅 (奈良県)|桜井駅]]よりバス）ないし徒歩（上記ハイキングコースの場合、石舞台から1.5〜2時間程度）の利用が推奨される。
== ギャラリー == 多武峰街道 <gallery> 画像:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|摩尼輪塔 画像:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|三社神社 画像:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|磨崖仏 画像:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|竹林 画像:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|不動の滝 画像:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|屋形橋 </gallery>
== その他 == * この付近にあるバス停の名前は、多武「'''峯'''」と表記されている。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選]] * [[多武峯少将物語]]
{{mountain-stub|pref=奈良県}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とうのみね}} [[Category:桜井市]] [[Category:奈良県の地理]]

revision.text:
'''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|ja|桜井市}}, Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}, a devotee of {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}} records in {{ill|858|ja|858年}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社}} or possibly {{ill|Abo-yama Kofun|ja|阿武山古墳}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the completion of the fully two-lane {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 155|ja|奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線}} in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|ja|桜井駅}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|ja|美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選}} * {{ill|Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari|ja|多武峯少将物語}}

'''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|ja|桜井市}}, Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}, a devotee of {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}} records in {{ill|858|ja|858年}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社}} or possibly {{ill|Abo-yama Kofun|ja|阿武山古墳}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the completion of the fully two-lane {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 155|ja|奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線}} in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|ja|桜井駅}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>

'''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|ja|桜井市}}, Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}, a devotee of {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}} records in {{ill|858|ja|858年}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社}} or possibly {{ill|Abo-yama Kofun|ja|阿武山古墳}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the completion of the fully two-lane {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 155|ja|奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線}} in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|ja|桜井駅}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|ja|美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選}} * {{ill|Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari|ja|多武峯少将物語}}

Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}} records in {{ill|858|ja|858年}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社}} or possibly {{ill|Abo-yama Kofun|ja|阿武山古墳}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: of the fully two-lane {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 155|ja|奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線}} in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|ja|桜井駅}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).

revision.text:
'''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|ja|桜井市}}, Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}, a devotee of {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}} records in {{ill|858|ja|858年}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社}} or possibly {{ill|Abo-yama Kofun|ja|阿武山古墳}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺}}

This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: of the fully two-lane {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 155|ja|奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線}} in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|ja|桜井駅}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道}}.

revision.text:
'''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|ja|桜井市|12=draft}}, Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇|12=draft}}, a devotee of {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|12=draft}}, is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}.<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}} records in {{ill|858|ja|858th year|12=draft}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国|12=draft}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=draft}} or possibly {{ill|Abo-yama Kofun|ja|阿武山古墳|12=draft}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社|12=draft}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=draft}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=draft}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=draft}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=draft}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the completion tier 6]]
<!--Mount Tonomine-->
<!--Mount Tonomine-->
<!--Mount Tonomine-->
{{draft categories| [[Category: tier 6]] [[Category: of the fully two-lane {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 155|ja|奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線|12=draft}} in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳|12=draft}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|ja|桜井駅|12=draft}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=draft}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the completion tier 6]]
<!--Mount Tonomine-->
<!--Mount Tonomine-->
<!--Mount Tonomine-->
{{draft categories| [[Category: of the fully two-lane {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 155|ja|奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線|12=draft}} in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳|12=draft}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|ja|桜井駅|12=draft}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|ja|美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari|ja|多武峯少将物語|12=draft}}
{{mountain-stub|pref=Nara}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Sakurai, Nara]] [[Category:Geography of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Mount Tonomine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Mount Tonomine]] [[ja:多武峰]] [[en:Mount Tonomine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Mount Tonomine}}

'''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|ja|桜井市|12=draft}}, Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇|12=draft}}, a devotee of {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|12=draft}}, is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}.<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}} records in {{ill|858|ja|858th year|12=draft}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国|12=draft}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=draft}} or possibly {{ill|Abo-yama Kofun|ja|阿武山古墳|12=draft}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社|12=draft}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=draft}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=draft}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=draft}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=draft}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the completion tier 6]]

<!--Mount Tonomine-->
{{draft categories| [[Category: of the fully two-lane {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 155|ja|奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線|12=draft}} in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳|12=draft}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|ja|桜井駅|12=draft}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).

revision.text:
'''多武峰'''（とうのみね）は[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]南部にある山、および、その一帯にあった寺院のこと。
[[飛鳥時代]]に[[道教]]を信奉していた[[斉明天皇]]が、『日本書紀』に、「多武峰の山頂付近に石塁や高殿を築いて'''両槻宮'''（ふたつきのみや）とした」とある<ref>『[[日本書紀]]』では両槻宮を「観」と表現し、道教寺院を「道観」ということから道教施設説もあるが、「観」には単に「高殿」の意味もあるため、[[遠山美都男]]は疑問が残るとし、別名の「天宮」に関しても、道教[[仙人]]の住まう宮殿の意味だが、[[仏教]]の[[帝釈天]]が住む「忉利天宮」の略称の可能性も出す。[[遠山美都男]]『天皇と日本の起源「飛鳥の大王」の謎を解く』（[[講談社現代新書]]、2003年）p.186.</ref>。『日本三代実録』に、[[858年]]（天安2年）「多武峰墓を藤原鎌足の墓とし、十陵四墓の例に入れる」と記されている。平安時代中頃の成立と見られる『多武峯略記』に、「最初は摂津国安威（現在の大阪府茨木市大織冠神社、[[阿武山古墳]]か）に葬られたが、後に大和国の多武峯に改葬された」との説が見える。
明治初年の[[神仏分離]]により、[[談山神社]]となった。詳細は、[[談山神社]]を参照。'''多武峰街道'''は談山神社から等弥神社を経て[[山の辺の道]]に接続する。
== 社寺 == * [[談山神社]] * [[等弥神社]] * [[聖林寺]]
== 交通 == [[Image:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|県道155線。左側に歩行者への注意を促す標識がある]] 自動車の場合、従来は桜井方面より[[奈良県道37号桜井吉野線]]を上るしかなかったが、すれ違い困難な一車線区間が多く、紅葉シーズンの渋滞は激しい。2009年に全線二車線の[[奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線]]が開通し、飛鳥方面からのアクセスが飛躍的に向上したが、[[石舞台古墳|石舞台]]からのハイキングコースと数カ所で交差しており、走行に注意が必要である。いずれにせよ、多武峰の駐車場キャパシティに限界があるので、ハイシーズンは公共交通機関（[[桜井駅 (奈良県)|桜井駅]]よりバス）ないし徒歩（上記ハイキングコースの場合、石舞台から1.5〜2時間程度）の利用が推奨される。
== ギャラリー == 多武峰街道 <gallery> 画像:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|摩尼輪塔 画像:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|三社神社 画像:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|磨崖仏 画像:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|竹林 画像:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|不動の滝 画像:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|屋形橋 </gallery>
== 表記 == * この付近にあるバス停の名称は「峰」の[[異体字]]「峯」を用いた「多武'''峯'''」になっている。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選]] * [[多武峯少将物語]]
{{mountain-stub|pref=奈良県}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とうのみね}} [[Category:桜井市]] [[Category:奈良県の地理]]

== ギャラリー == 多武峰街道 <gallery> 画像:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|摩尼輪塔 画像:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|三社神社 画像:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|磨崖仏 画像:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|竹林 画像:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|不動の滝 画像:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|屋形橋 </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11430808]] Wikidata name: Mount Tōnomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]], a devotee of [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]], is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|qq=|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|qq=|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from [[:en:Sakurai Station|Sakurai Station (Nara)]]) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11430808]] Wikidata name: Mount Tōnomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.

While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|qq=|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|qq=|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from [[:en:Sakurai Station|Sakurai Station (Nara)]]) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430808]] Wikidata name: Mount Tōnomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]], a devotee of [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]], is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.

in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from [[:en:Sakurai Station|Sakurai Station (Nara)]]) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).

== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

During the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]], a devotee of [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]], is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.

For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from [[:en:Sakurai Station|Sakurai Station (Nara)]]) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430808]] Wikidata name: Mount Tōnomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]], a devotee of [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]], is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.

Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from [[:en:Sakurai Station|Sakurai Station (Nara)]]) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430808]] Wikidata name: Mount Tōnomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]], a devotee of [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]], is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.

This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from [[:en:Sakurai Station|Sakurai Station (Nara)]]) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430808]] Wikidata name: Mount Tōnomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]], a devotee of [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]], is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from [[:en:Sakurai Station|Sakurai Station (Nara)]]) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|ja|美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|1=500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871795}} * [[:en:Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari|Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari]]
{{mountain-stub|pref=Nara}}
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430808]] Wikidata name: Mount Tōnomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]], a devotee of [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]], is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from [[:en:Sakurai Station|Sakurai Station (Nara)]]) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|ja|美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|1=500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871795}} * [[:en:Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari|Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari]]
{{mountain-stub|pref=Nara}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430808]] Wikidata name: Mount Tōnomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]], a devotee of [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]], is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] records in [[:en:858|858]] (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly [[:en:Abuyama Kofun|Abuyama Kofun]], but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] as part of the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]] * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]], necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from [[:en:Sakurai Station|Sakurai Station (Nara)]]) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).
== References == {{Reflist}}
