revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430808]] Wikidata name: Mount Tōnomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Saimei}}, a devotee of {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}, is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}.<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}} records in {{ill|858|en|858|WD=Q30371|lt=858}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly {{ill|Abuyama Kofun|en|Abuyama Kofun|WD=Q11657465|lt=Abuyama Kofun}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station|en|Sakurai Station|WD=Q1205698|lt=Sakurai Station (Nara)}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

'''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Saimei}}, a devotee of {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}, is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}.<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}} records in {{ill|858|en|858|WD=Q30371|lt=858}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly {{ill|Abuyama Kofun|en|Abuyama Kofun|WD=Q11657465|lt=Abuyama Kofun}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station|en|Sakurai Station|WD=Q1205698|lt=Sakurai Station (Nara)}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).

Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}} records in {{ill|858|en|858|WD=Q30371|lt=858}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly {{ill|Abuyama Kofun|en|Abuyama Kofun|WD=Q11657465|lt=Abuyama Kofun}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station|en|Sakurai Station|WD=Q1205698|lt=Sakurai Station (Nara)}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]]
'''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Saimei}}, a devotee of {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}, is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}.<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}} records in {{ill|858|en|858|WD=Q30371|lt=858}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly {{ill|Abuyama Kofun|en|Abuyama Kofun|WD=Q11657465|lt=Abuyama Kofun}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station|en|Sakurai Station|WD=Q1205698|lt=Sakurai Station (Nara)}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).

== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun|1=Ishibutai Kofun}}, necessitating careful driving. == Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).

{{draft categories| <!--Mount Tonomine--><!--Mount Tonomine--><!--Mount Tonomine--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Mount Tōnomine (Nara)}} {{wikidata link|Q11430808}} [[Category: of the fully two-lane {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 155|ja|奈良県道155号多武峯見瀬線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 155|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 155|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445281}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Mount Tōnomine (Nara)]][[ja:多武峰]]
'''Tōnomine''' (多武峰) refers to a mountain located in the southern part of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture, as well as the temple complex that once existed in the surrounding area.
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|WD=Q235296|lt=Empress Saimei|1=Empress Kōgyoku}}, a devotee of {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}}, is said to have built stone fortifications and a grand hall near the summit of Tōnomine, naming it the "Palace of Futatsuki" (両槻宮), according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}.<ref>In the Nihon Shoki, the term "観" (kan) is used to describe the Palace of Futatsuki. While it may suggest a Taoist temple ("道観"), it can also mean a "grand hall." Historian Mitsuo Tōyama argues that this ambiguity leaves room for debate. Additionally, the alternative name "Heavenly Palace" (天宮) might refer to the Taoist immortal’s celestial abode or could be a reference to the Buddhist Trayastrimsa Heaven (忉利天宮). Mitsuo Tōyama, *The Origins of the Japanese Emperor: Solving the Mysteries of the Great Kings of Asuka*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, p. 186.</ref> Furthermore, the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}} records in {{ill|858|en|858|WD=Q30371|lt=858|1=858}} (Ten'an 2) that "Tōnomine was identified as the tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari and included among the 'Ten Imperial Tombs and Four Graves.'" A mid-Heian-period document, *Tōnomine Ryakki* (多武峯略記), states that Kamatari was originally buried in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}, at the current site of {{ill|Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja|大織冠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtorikabuto Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or possibly {{ill|Abuyama Kofun|en|Abuyama Kofun|WD=Q11657465|lt=Abuyama Kofun|1=Abuyama Kofun}}, but was later reinterred at Tōnomine in Yamato Province.
In the early Meiji period, the temple complex was transformed into {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine|1=Tanzan Shrine}} as part of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} policies. For more details, see the article on Tanzan Shrine. The "Tōnomine Highway" connects Tanzan Shrine to {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine (Q11602724)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} and intersects with the {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山の辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山辺の道}}.
== Shrines and Temples == * {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine|1=Tanzan Shrine}} * {{ill|Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja|等弥神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomimiyashiro Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 等彌神社|1=Tomi Shrine (Q11602724)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602724}} * {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Nara)|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Nara)|1=Shōrin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611064}}
== Access == [[File:NaraPrefRoad155.JPG|thumb|Prefectural Route 155, with a sign urging caution for pedestrians.]] For those traveling by car, access was previously limited to ascending from the Sakurai side via {{ill|Nara Prefectural Route 37|ja|奈良県道37号桜井吉野線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefectural Route 37|1=Nara Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445381}}. This route included many narrow, single-lane sections, causing severe congestion during the autumn foliage season. However, the Completion tier 6]]
{{translated page|ja|多武峰|version=100113511|comment=Imported full ja history}}
in 2009 significantly improved access from the Asuka area. Nonetheless, this road intersects several hiking trails, including one leading from the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun|1=Ishibutai Kofun}}, necessitating careful driving. Parking at Tōnomine remains limited, so visitors are advised to use public transportation (buses from {{ill|Sakurai Station|en|Sakurai Station|WD=Q1205698|lt=Sakurai Station (Nara)|1=Sakurai Station}}) or hike (approximately 1.5–2 hours from Ishibutai Kofun).
== Gallery == Tōnomine Highway <gallery> File:Manirijnto01s3200.jpg|Manirin-tō Pagoda File:Tounomine01s3200.jpg|Sansha Shrine File:Tounomine02s3200.jpg|Magaibutsu Buddhist Carvings File:Tounomine03s3200.jpg|Bamboo Forest File:Fudounotaki_tonomine.JPG|Fudō Waterfall File:Yakatabashi tonomine.JPG|Yakata Bridge </gallery>
== Naming == The nearby bus stop uses the variant character 峯 ("Mine") in its name, rendering it as "Tōnomine" (多武峯).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|ja|美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|1=500 Beautiful Paths to Walk in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871795}} * {{ill|Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari|en|Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari|WD=Q3818194|lt=Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari|1=Tōnomine Shōshō Monogatari}}
